does: This paper audits collateral knowledge damage in large language model unlearning before the unlearning procedure is executed. The authors empirically study how unlearning effects propagate across knowledge domains, discovering a consistent decay pattern where damage is strongest adjacent to the forget set, diminishes with semantic distance, but persists across domain boundaries. They formulate forget-set auditing as a predictive task and identify which data features best anticipate downstream collateral damage. The key finding is that interaction features between the forget set and evaluation set provide the strongest predictive signals, indicating that damage patterns are reflected in data geometry before model updates occur, enabling pre-execution risk assessment.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts in machine unlearning for language models, which emerged as a response to privacy and safety requirements in large-scale NLP systems. It extends prior work on measuring unlearning effects beyond simple forget-set performance to study the broader ecosystem of related knowledge that unlearning disrupts. The paper connects to research on knowledge entanglement in neural networks and the difficulty of surgical information removal, while adopting a data-centric lens inspired by modern machine learning practices that emphasize dataset analysis. It relates to work on understanding model behavior through feature interaction analysis and connects to emerging literature on auditing and interpretability methods that assess model properties without full retraining.
enables: This work enables the development of more controlled unlearning procedures by providing pre-execution diagnostics of collateral damage risk. The auditing framework could facilitate safer unlearning by allowing practitioners to estimate potential harm before committing computational resources to model updates. Future work could build predictive models that estimate damage extent and guide unlearning algorithm design to minimize collateral effects. The discovered decay pattern and feature interaction insights suggest directions for developing targeted regularization or constraint-based unlearning methods that preserve semantically distant knowledge. More broadly, this enables a quality-assurance infrastructure for unlearning that mirrors safety validation practices in other domains, making unlearning more practical for real-world deployment where knowledge removal consequences must be carefully managed.
